rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute secondary cellulitis on a chronic venous leg ulcer in a systemically-well patient (afebrile, normal heart rate, denies rigors, no proximal spread). This warrants urgent SAME-DAY in-person assessment (T3): the wound needs professional cleansing and an absorbent dressing, oral antibiotics need to be started, arterial supply must be checked before any compression, and deep-tissue involvement or osteomyelitis cannot be excluded over telehealth. It is not an ED/000 emergency (no systemic sepsis, no rapid spread, no necrotising features), so T4/T5 would be over-triage; but routine GP or self-care would dangerously under-treat an active spreading infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness spreading away from the ulcer up the calf towards the knee, or the surrounding cellulitis visibly enlarging
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — seek urgent in-person/ED review if the redness is advancing
escalation_edges.rationale: Proximally spreading cellulitis indicates the infection is not controlled and may need IV antibiotics and hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Draw a pen line at the edge of the redness. If it spreads past that line up your calf towards your knee, go to the emergency department or an urgent care clinic the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever, uncontrollable shivering/rigors, the whole leg becoming swollen and severely painful, or feeling generally very unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of systemic sepsis from spreading infection — a time-critical emergency needing ambulance transfer and IV therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever with uncontrollable shivering, or the whole leg swells up and becomes severely painful, or you feel very unwell, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain rapidly becoming far more severe than the skin appearance suggests, the skin turning dusky/purple or blistering, or a spreading area of numbness
escalation_edges.rationale: Pain out of proportion, skin necrosis/blistering or crepitus suggest a necrotising soft-tissue infection — a surgical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly becomes much worse than the wound looks, or the skin turns dusky, purple or blistered, call 000 immediately — do not wait.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with reduced mobility and disturbed sleep from the painful leg
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Ensure the same-day in-person pathway is genuinely accessible (arrange transport or a home/community wound-nursing visit if needed), confirm she has someone to check on her, and make the deterioration triggers explicit and written down since no one else is present to notice them.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Chronic venous insufficiency with lipodermatosclerosis; compression stockings stopped for a week
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise elevation now and defer compression until infection settles and arterial supply is checked; flag the non-adherence as a reversible driver of the flare to address at follow-up.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This needs to be seen in person today. Arrange an urgent same-day appointment with your GP, an urgent care clinic, or an after-hours/community wound service to have the wound cleaned and dressed and to start antibiotics for the skin infection. Until then, keep the leg raised above heart level, keep the current dressing clean and dry, and do not put your compression stockings back on.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP / urgent care / after-hours or community wound service; call 000 if emergency features develop
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If your GP is closed, use an after-hours GP service, a community/after-hours wound-nursing service, or an urgent care clinic today rather than waiting; do not leave an actively infected wound untreated overnight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Acute secondary cellulitis on a chronic medial-malleolar venous leg ulcer, systemically well; commenced/for oral flucloxacillin, absorbent dressing, strict elevation, compression deferred pending ABPI; community wound-care referral and 48-hour review arranged.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as an unchanged chronic ulcer and giving self-care or routine (1-2 week) GP advice, missing the acute cellulitis.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated spreading cellulitis progresses to systemic sepsis or deep-tissue infection requiring hospital admission for IV antibiotics.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending a prompt (24-48 h) GP appointment but not same-day in-person wound care/antibiotics for an actively infected, heavily exuding wound that cannot be dressed remotely.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a systemically-well, afebrile patient with localised cellulitis to ED/000, consuming emergency resources when urgent same-day in-person primary/wound care is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure vital signs, palpate pulses, gauge the true extent of the cellulitic margin, or exclude deep-tissue involvement/osteomyelitis.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Default to urgent same-day in-person assessment for objective examination and wound care, start antibiotics through that pathway, and safety-net explicitly for the deterioration signs that cannot be examined for remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either falsely reassuring and managing the wound remotely, or over-escalating a systemically-well patient to an ambulance.
